source: Limitless (Bankless) — AI supercycle; Unity as world-model builder; chips/memory/power
summary: Sovereign/physical AI infrastructure deployment speed is a durable moat — companies that can build fastest win
summary_extended: Baker argues that the speed of physical AI infrastructure deployment — spanning data centers, power procurement, and chip installation — constitutes a durable structural moat in the AI supercycle. His reasoning is that hardware complexity, supply chain constraints, and grid limitations make rapid buildout extraordinarily difficult, meaning early movers who can execute at speed lock in advantages that are hard to replicate. He cites SpaceX's Colossus supercluster as a concrete example of a fast-deployer monetizing this capability by renting compute to Anthropic, demonstrating that deployment speed itself can be commercialized. The investment implication is to favor companies with demonstrated ability to build AI infrastructure rapidly at scale, though Baker's primary exemplar (SpaceX) is private, making direct public-market expression of this thesis difficult. No specific public-market tickers were named or clearly implied with sufficient specificity to add without speculation.
detail: Baker identifies the speed of physical deployment (data centers, power, chips) as a structural moat because hardware complexity and grid constraints make rapid buildout extremely difficult. SpaceX/Colossus is cited as an example of a fast-deployer monetizing this capability by renting compute to Anthropic.
confidence_evidence: Named as one of four key investment pillars; SpaceX cited as exemplar but is private
quote_fragment: speed of physical deployment itself is the moat
